title: Walnut end-grain chopping board
subtitle: An end-grain cutting board assembled from walnut, cherry, and maple offcuts
excerpt: An end-grain chopping board presents the wood fibres upright to the knife blade, which parts them and closes again rather than cutting across them; the result is a surface that is kinder to knife edges and self-healing at the surface level. The board is assembled from short offcuts of contrasting hardwoods glued up in a grid, then sanded through the grits to a board-butter finish. This beginner project covers the glue-up sequence and the sanding and finishing steps.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for joinery glue-up technique. Encyclopaedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) for hardwood species and their kitchen uses.

content.text: An
end-grain

board is harder to make than a face-grain board but much better to use: the fibres close after each knife stroke, the surface remains clean much longer, and the board is significantly kinder to a sharp blade edge. The contrasting colour pattern from alternating walnut and maple or cherry offcuts is also attractive. All stock must be
seasoned
and dry before the glue-up; any moisture in the wood will cause the joints to open as the board dries.
What you need: wood

Short offcuts of hardwood, all the same thickness (30 mm is a practical working thickness for a kitchen board). Walnut, cherry, and maple are the traditional combination; oak is an alternative but its tannins can react with some glues. The total amount of wood depends on board size: a 30 cm by 25 cm board needs approximately 30 offcut strips, each 25 cm long, 30 mm thick, and 20 to 25 mm wide. All must be planed flat and square on all four long faces before the glue-up.
What you need: tools and materials

Food-safe wood glue (PVA or a waterproof equivalent such as Titebond III). Bar clamps or sash clamps for the glue-up (not included in the tool list as large fixed shop tools; a set of 4 clamps minimum). Sandpaper in 80, 120, 180, 240, 320, and 400 grit.
Board butter
for the finish.
Safety
Glue squeeze-out on skin is not hazardous but remove it before it sets. Do not use boiled linseed oil on a food-contact surface; board butter or raw linseed oil is the appropriate food-safe finish.
The work
Step 1: Arrange and glue the strips

Arrange the strips in the intended colour pattern: alternate walnut and maple, or a repeating sequence of walnut, cherry, maple. Apply glue to all mating faces, reassemble in sequence, and clamp with bar clamps across the full width. Wipe off squeeze-out immediately with a damp cloth. Leave in clamps for 24 hours at room temperature before removing.
Step 2: Flatten the panel

After the glue has fully set (48 hours minimum), the panel can be flattened. The most accessible hand-tool method is belt sanding or hand sanding with 80 grit on a flat block across the full surface. Work in a circular motion to level any slight thickness variation between strips. The end grain will sand more slowly than the face grain of any long-grain strips in the assembly; take extra passes over any high areas.
Step 3: Sand through the grits

Sand the full surface through 80, 120, 180, 240, 320, and 400 grit, using a flat sanding block throughout. Change direction by 45 degrees between grits to make the scratches from the previous grit visible; sand until the previous-grit scratches are gone before moving to the next grit. End grain takes longer to sand smooth than face grain at each stage; do not rush the transition from 120 to 180 grit, where the surface character is established.
Step 4: Apply board butter

Apply board butter generously to all surfaces including the underside. Rub in with a cloth, leave for 5 minutes, then buff to a dry surface. The end grain will absorb more than the face grain; apply a second coat to any surface that still looks dry after buffing. Board butter is food-safe immediately.
Variations
A face-grain board uses the same wood layout, but with the strips lying flat rather than on end. Face-grain boards are faster to make and easier to flatten, but they wear faster in use.

Add feet to the underside of the board. Four small silicone pads or turned hardwood buttons work. Feet stop the board sliding on the worktop. They also let air flow under the board, which keeps the underside dry.
Care
Hand-wash only; never put in a dishwasher. Re-apply board butter every few months or when the surface looks dry. Store upright rather than flat to prevent moisture accumulation on one face.

items.fix: Leave in clamps for 48 hours rather than 24. If a joint has already opened, clean out the gap, re-glue with a syringe if necessary, and re-clamp.
items.cause: The mating faces were not fully flat, or the glue did not set fully before unclamping.
items.symptom: A glue joint opens after the clamps are removed

items.fix: Return to the previous grit and sand until the current scratches are uniform, then move up. End grain is the critical area; it always needs more passes than face grain at each transition.
items.cause: The grit transition was made before all scratches from the previous grit were removed.
items.symptom: The sanded surface shows different colours at each grit level
